title: Tamam Shud: Somerton Man Identified in 2022 🔍
segments: On December 1, 1948, on Somerton Beach near Adelaide, Australia, a man was found dead leaning against a seawall. He had no identification. The labels on his clothing had been cut out. The case would remain unsolved for the next 73 years.

Locals had seen him the previous evening — apparently asleep against the wall — and assumed he was a drunk dozing in the sea air. By morning he was cold. South Australia Police were called at sunrise.

The autopsy by Dr. John Dwyer at Royal Adelaide Hospital found unusual signs. The man's organs were congested. His spleen was enlarged. His liver showed evidence of acute toxicity. No conventional poison could be identified at the time, but Dwyer suspected one had been used.

The man was approximately 40 years old. He was 5 feet 11 inches tall, fit, well-dressed in a brown suit, and had calluses suggesting work as a manual laborer or possibly as a dancer. His teeth showed unusual wear patterns.

Police found a half-smoked cigarette resting on his collar. They found a comb, a matchbook, used train tickets to Henley Beach, an unused bus ticket, and chewing gum. They found no wallet, no ID, no money.

Here's where the case turned strange. In a hidden fob pocket of his trousers, investigators discovered a tiny scrap of paper. It had been carefully torn from a book. It contained two words in Persian script — 'Tamam Shud.'

'Tamam Shud' is the final phrase from the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam, the medieval Persian poet whose work had been popular in English translation for nearly a century. It means roughly 'finished' or 'ended.'

South Australia Police asked the public to help find the book the page was torn from. After a public appeal, a man came forward. He had found a copy of the Rubaiyat tossed into the back seat of his unlocked car parked near Somerton Beach.

Inside that book, investigators found two crucial things. First, the back page was missing — the page from which 'Tamam Shud' had been torn. Second, written in pencil on the inside back cover were five lines of letters that appeared to be a code.
The code became known as the Somerton code. It has resisted decryption for over 70 years. Cryptographers from the Australian military, GCHQ, the CIA, and university teams worldwide have tried.

Also written in the book was a phone number. It belonged to Jessica Thomson, a young nurse who lived a short walk from Somerton Beach. When police interviewed her, she insisted she did not know the dead man.

Thomson became one of the most studied figures in the case. Her demeanor when shown photographs of the body was described by detectives as visibly shaken. She asked never to be named publicly. Her name was suppressed in court records for decades.

The Somerton man was buried in Adelaide's West Terrace Cemetery in June 1949. The plaster bust of his face, made by sculptor Paul Lawson, was placed on his grave. The bust would prove crucial decades later.
For 73 years, the case sat unresolved. Theories ranged from Cold War espionage to an estranged lover to a deserter from a wartime army. None were ever proved.

In 2009, Adelaide University researcher Derek Abbott began an academic project on the case. He noticed that Jessica Thomson's son Robin had unusual ear and dental features that matched the Somerton man's photographs.
Abbott pursued the link for over a decade. He contacted descendants. He pushed for exhumation. South Australia Police and government refused multiple times.
In 2021, after years of advocacy, South Australia Attorney-General Vickie Chapman approved exhumation of the body. In May 2021, the remains were carefully removed from West Terrace Cemetery.

Forensic teams at Flinders University extracted DNA from teeth and bone. They sent samples to genealogy databases. In July 2022, Abbott and forensic scientist Colleen Fitzpatrick announced they had identified the Somerton man.

His name was Carl Charles Webb. He was born November 16, 1905, in Footscray, a suburb of Melbourne. He was an electrical engineer and instrument maker. He had been married to Dorothy Robertson but the marriage had collapsed in the late 1940s.

Webb had a history of writing poetry. He had recited the Rubaiyat in his youth. The book and the torn phrase 'Tamam Shud' — meaning 'ended' — fit a man who had walked deliberately to a beach, taken poison, and left the page as a final note.

South Australia Police accepted the identification but have not officially closed the inquest. They continue to investigate the cause of death. Modern toxicology cannot examine remains 73 years after death with full certainty.

The Somerton code remains unsolved. Some researchers now believe it may be personal — initials, dates, or fragments meaningful only to Webb. Others maintain it is a true cipher with espionage origins.
Jessica Thomson's link to Webb has not been definitively proven. Her son Robin has died. DNA from his descendants has been compared. Results have not been publicly confirmed.

Carl Webb's family in Melbourne accepted his identification quietly. After more than seven decades, his absence from the family record now has an explanation, even if the full circumstances of his death do not.

If you are researching the case, the Adelaide University Tamam Shud Project maintains a public archive of documents, photographs, and code analyses. The South Australia Police inquest file is partly open to researchers.
